prompt:
A city installed cameras at several intersections, and over the following year the number of reported accidents at those intersections fell by 20%. The mayor concluded that the cameras made drivers more careful, thereby reducing accidents.

Which of the following, if true, most weakens the mayor's conclusion?
choices: At the camera intersections, accidents dropped most sharply in the first weeks after the cameras were switched on.
The intersections selected for cameras were among the highest-traffic intersections anywhere in the city.
Citywide that year, including at intersections without cameras, reported accidents fell by roughly the same 20%.
Installing and maintaining the cameras cost the city less than the accidents they were projected to prevent.
In a survey, most local drivers said they had noticed the newly installed cameras at those intersections.
exp: A citywide decline of the same magnitude, including where no cameras were installed, points to a general trend rather than the cameras as the cause. The timing detail in the first option would support the conclusion, not weaken it.
creator: Claude Sonnet 5

prompt:
In a three-month trial, patients taking a new drug had lower cholesterol at the end than at the start. The manufacturer concluded that the drug lowers cholesterol.

Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the conclusion?
choices: Patients who took the drug reported feeling generally healthier by the end of the trial.
During the trial, the patients also began exercising considerably more than before it started.
The drug costs noticeably less than other cholesterol treatments now on the market.
Cholesterol levels are known to drift up and down somewhat from one month to the next.
A comparable placebo group showed no drop in cholesterol over the same three months.
exp: If an untreated comparison group shows no decline over the same period, natural fluctuation and the passage of time are ruled out, leaving the drug as the cause. Added exercise would instead weaken the argument by supplying an alternative cause.
creator: Claude Sonnet 5

prompt:
A legal-aid clinic represents tenants in eviction cases and reports that 80% of its clients avoid eviction—far above the citywide rate for tenants facing eviction. The clinic's director concludes that the clinic's representation is highly effective at preventing evictions.

The director's conclusion depends on assuming which of the following?
choices: The clinic handles a substantially larger number of eviction cases each year than any other comparable organization in the city.
The cases the clinic takes on are not, as a group, easier to win than eviction cases citywide.
Tenants the clinic represents are generally satisfied with the quality of its service.
The citywide rate at which tenants facing eviction are removed has been rising in recent years.
The clinic's attorneys are paid less than attorneys doing comparable work in private practice.
exp: Negation test: if the clinic mainly accepts cases that are already easier to win, its high success rate reflects which cases it selects rather than how well it represents them, and the conclusion collapses, so the argument must assume otherwise. Client satisfaction or caseload volume would round out the story but are not required.
creator: Claude Sonnet 5

prompt:
Every employee who completed the company's leadership program was promoted within two years. The head of training concludes that completing the program causes employees to be promoted.

The reasoning is most vulnerable to the criticism that it
choices: bases a broad causal claim on a sample of employees far too small to support any general conclusion
assumes that the employees in question actually regarded being promoted as desirable
treats a policy that is still relatively new as though its effects were already permanent
ignores that employees already likely to be promoted may be the ones admitted to the program
fails to weigh the financial cost of running the program against the benefits it produces
exp: If the people already on track for promotion are the ones selected for the program, then completing it need not cause the promotions; the argument overlooks this self-selection. Sample size and cost sound like flaws but are not the error the reasoning actually commits.
creator: Claude Sonnet 5

prompt:
All of the firm's senior analysts have completed the certification. No one who has completed the certification has fewer than five years of experience.

If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true?
choices: Anyone at the firm with five years of experience is a senior analyst.
Some of the firm's senior analysts have not completed the certification.
Every senior analyst has at least five years of experience.
No analyst below senior level has five years of experience.
Only senior analysts have completed the certification.
exp: Senior analysts are all certified, and everyone certified has at least five years' experience; chaining the two forces the conclusion that every senior analyst has at least five years. The other options reverse the conditionals or add claims the premises do not support.
creator: Claude Sonnet 5

prompt:
A school district replaced printed textbooks with tablets, and the following year students' average test scores rose. A district official credits the switch to tablets.

The answer to which of the following would be most useful in evaluating the official's claim?
choices: Whether comparable districts that kept printed textbooks saw a similar score rise that year
Whether students reported preferring the new tablets to the printed textbooks they had replaced
Whether the tablets ultimately cost the district more than the textbooks they replaced
Whether teachers received any formal training in how to use the new tablets
Whether the rise in scores was larger in some grade levels than in others
exp: If comparable districts that kept textbooks saw the same rise, then something other than the tablets explains it, so that comparison directly tests whether the tablets were the cause. Training, cost, or preference may matter for other reasons but do not isolate the tablets' effect.
creator: Claude Sonnet 5

prompt:
After a company doubled its advertising budget, surveys showed that brand recognition among consumers rose substantially. Yet the company's sales over the same period remained essentially flat.

Which of the following, if true, best explains this result?
choices: The new advertisements ran during television programs that consistently draw some of the largest audiences nationwide.
The company's products are widely regarded as well made and reliable.
Brand recognition was measured by surveying a sample of ordinary consumers.
Competitors raised their own advertising just as sharply over the same period, leaving relative share unchanged.
In the year before, the company had spent comparatively little on advertising.
exp: If rival firms increased their advertising just as much, the company's recognition could climb while its position relative to competitors, and therefore its sales, stayed flat. The other choices add detail about the ads or products but do not explain why higher recognition failed to lift sales.
creator: Claude Sonnet 5

prompt:
Some economists argue that raising tariffs protects domestic jobs. (1) It is true that tariffs can preserve employment in the protected industries in the short run. However, (2) tariffs also raise input costs for domestic manufacturers that depend on imported materials, which can eliminate jobs elsewhere. The overall effect on employment is therefore uncertain.

In the argument, the two portions marked (1) and (2) play which of the following roles?
choices: (1) is the author's main conclusion, and (2) provides further support for that conclusion.
(1) and (2) are both claims that the author ultimately rejects as mistaken.
(1) is a claim the author grants, and (2) is a consideration used to qualify it.
(1) states the author's main conclusion, and (2) is evidence offered against it.
(1) and (2) are both pieces of evidence supporting the economists' original position.
exp: The author concedes (1), that tariffs can protect some jobs in the short run, then introduces (2) to qualify it and reach the guarded conclusion that the net effect is uncertain. So (1) is a granted point rather than a conclusion, and (2) qualifies rather than supports it.
creator: Claude Sonnet 5

prompt:
A new bridge will shorten commute times, the transit authority argues, because it provides a more direct route between the suburbs and the downtown business district.

The argument depends on assuming which of the following?
choices: The new bridge is more visually appealing than the route commuters currently take.
The downtown business district is expected to keep expanding steadily over the next several years.
Construction of the new bridge was finished on or ahead of the planned schedule.
The suburbs the bridge connects contain a substantial number of daily commuters.
Commuters will in fact use the new bridge instead of staying on their current routes.
exp: Negation test: if commuters keep to their existing routes, the more direct bridge saves no one any time and the conclusion fails, so the argument must assume they will switch. Schedule, growth, and the number of commuters make the scenario feel complete but are not needed for the time-saving conclusion.
creator: Claude Sonnet 5

prompt:
Office workers who choose to take a short walk at midday report higher afternoon productivity than workers who do not. A manager concludes that requiring all employees to take a midday walk would raise overall productivity.

Which of the following, if true, most weakens the manager's conclusion?
choices: Those who choose to walk may already be more energetic and motivated, which itself raises productivity.
A mandatory midday walk is something that a substantial number of the company's employees say they would actively resent.
Walking at midday carries health benefits that have little to do with productivity.
The higher afternoon productivity of the walkers was measured entirely by self-report.
The comparison between walkers and non-walkers covered a span of only two weeks.
exp: If the employees who choose to walk are already more energetic or driven, those traits rather than the walk may explain their productivity, so requiring everyone to walk need not reproduce the effect. Resentment, self-reporting, and the short window raise doubts but do not as directly sever the causal link.
creator: Claude Sonnet 5

prompt:
A bookstore began offering free coffee to customers, and its monthly sales rose. The owner concluded that the free coffee caused the increase.

The conclusion depends on assuming which of the following?
choices: Customers who shop at the store generally prefer coffee to other free beverages such as tea.
No other change capable of lifting sales occurred at the same time the free coffee began.
Providing the free coffee was a significant ongoing expense for the store.
The store's monthly sales will continue to climb in the months ahead.
Most of the store's customers actually drank the free coffee that was offered.
exp: If something else that boosts sales, such as a promotion or a popular new release, changed at the same time, it rather than the coffee could account for the rise, so the argument must assume nothing else did. That most customers drank the coffee would help the story but is not what the causal conclusion strictly requires.
creator: Claude Sonnet 5

prompt:
A columnist notes that people who drink green tea daily tend to live longer than those who do not, and on that basis recommends green tea to extend lifespan.

Which of the following, if true, most weakens the recommendation?
choices: Green tea is known to contain several antioxidant compounds also found in many vegetables.
A number of people find the taste of green tea quite unappealing.
Green tea is inexpensive and widely available in most grocery stores.
Daily tea drinkers also tend to exercise more and smoke less than non-drinkers.
Lifespan is difficult to measure with any real precision.
exp: If habitual tea drinkers differ in other health-relevant habits, more exercise and less smoking, those differences rather than the tea may explain their longer lives, undercutting the recommendation. Antioxidant content or low price says nothing about whether the tea itself extends lifespan.
creator: Claude Sonnet 5

prompt:
Every product the company recalled last year was made at its Riverside plant. No product made at its Lakeside plant was recalled last year.

If the statements are true, which of the following must also be true?
choices: Every product made at the Riverside plant was recalled last year.
No product recalled last year was made at the Lakeside plant.
Lakeside makes more reliable products than Riverside does.
Most of the company's products are manufactured at Lakeside.
The Riverside plant will be shut down as a result.
exp: Since recalled products all came from Riverside and nothing from Lakeside was recalled, no recalled product could have been made at Lakeside. Claims about reliability, output share, or closing a plant go beyond what the two premises establish.
creator: Claude Sonnet 5

prompt:
A researcher claims a new teaching method improves retention, citing that students taught with it scored higher on a test given immediately after the lesson.

Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the claim that the method improves retention specifically?
choices: Students reported that they enjoyed the lessons taught with the new method.
The new method allowed the same material to be covered in noticeably less class time.
The immediate test that the students took used a multiple-choice format.
The teacher who used the new method happened to be unusually experienced.
Students taught with the method also scored higher on a test two months later.
exp: Retention is about how well learning lasts, so stronger performance on a test two months later supports improved retention rather than a momentary boost. An experienced teacher would instead supply an alternative explanation, and enjoyment or test format does not bear on the durability of learning.
creator: Claude Sonnet 5

prompt:
Critics claim a new policy failed because unemployment rose after it took effect. But unemployment also rose in neighboring regions that never adopted the policy, so the critics are wrong to blame it.

The argument is most vulnerable to the criticism that it
choices: overlooks that the policy may have limited a rise that would otherwise have been larger
depends entirely on the testimony of the very critics whose position it sets out to oppose
fails to settle on a precise definition of what should count as unemployment
relies on neighboring regions as the basis for its comparison
grants that unemployment did in fact rise after the policy took effect
exp: Showing that regions without the policy also worsened does not prove the policy had no effect; it may have softened the local increase relative to what would otherwise have occurred, a possibility the rebuttal ignores. Using neighbors as a comparison or conceding the rise are not themselves errors.
creator: Claude Sonnet 5

prompt:
A school introduced a free breakfast program, and over the next year the school's average test scores rose. Administrators concluded that eating breakfast improved the students' academic performance.

Which of the following, if true, most weakens the administrators' conclusion?
choices: The breakfast program proved popular with students and their families.
Students who ate the breakfast reported feeling more alert during morning classes.
In the same year, the school also reduced class sizes and hired additional tutors.
The food served in the program met national nutritional guidelines.
The school's test scores had been roughly flat for several years before the program began.
exp: If the school made other major changes at the same time—smaller classes, more tutoring—those could account for the score gains instead of the breakfast, undercutting the causal claim. The detail that scores were flat beforehand would, if anything, support the conclusion by ruling out a prior upward trend; the remaining options are consistent with the conclusion but establish no cause.
creator: Claude Sonnet 5

prompt:
A publisher plans to raise the cover price of its flagship magazine, expecting that the higher price per copy will increase the magazine's total revenue.

The publisher's plan depends on assuming which of the following?
choices: The magazine's production costs will fall once the new price takes effect.
Competing magazines will raise their cover prices by the same amount.
The higher price will attract entirely new categories of readers.
Raising the price will not reduce the number of copies sold so sharply that total revenue falls.
The magazine's existing readers are, on the whole, noticeably wealthier than the readers of its closest competitors.
exp: Negation test: if the price increase drove copies sold down enough that revenue fell, the plan would fail, so it must assume the drop in copies is not that severe. Falling costs would affect profit rather than the revenue claim; matching competitors, new reader categories, and reader wealth are not required for a higher price to raise revenue.
creator: Claude Sonnet 5

prompt:
Every product in the store's clearance aisle is discounted by at least 40 percent. No product discounted by 40 percent or more is eligible for the store's loyalty-points program.

If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true?
choices: Some products outside the clearance aisle are discounted by at least 40 percent.
Every product eligible for the loyalty-points program is located in the clearance aisle.
No product in the clearance aisle is eligible for the loyalty-points program.
The products in the clearance aisle are the store's most popular items.
Most products in the store are discounted by at least 40 percent.
exp: Clearance items are all discounted at least 40 percent, and nothing discounted at least 40 percent earns loyalty points, so no clearance item earns them—a valid chain. The claims about products outside the aisle, popularity, and store-wide discounting go beyond the premises, and the reversed conditional does not follow.
creator: Claude Sonnet 5

prompt: A car manufacturer introduced a new safety feature that automatically brakes when a collision is imminent. After equipping all new models with this feature, the manufacturer reported a 15% reduction in the number of insurance claims for accidents. The manufacturer concluded that the automatic braking system is effective in reducing accidents. Which of the following, if true, most weakens the manufacturer's conclusion?
choices: The insurance claims data includes claims for vandalism and theft, not just collisions.
During the same period, the overall speed limit in the region was reduced by 10 km/h.
The automatic braking system is optional on some models and many buyers chose not to purchase it.
The manufacturer's own testing showed that the system sometimes fails in heavy rain or fog.
Other car manufacturers also introduced similar systems at the same time.
exp: The conclusion that the braking system reduced accidents is weakened if another factor (e.g., lower speed limits) could have caused the reduction. Choice B provides an alternative cause. Choice A is tempting because it suggests the claims aren't all accident-related, but the conclusion is about accidents; if claims include non-accidents, the 15% reduction might not reflect accident reduction, but the argument is about 'claims for accidents,' so A may not apply if the data is specifically for accident claims. Choice D is a flaw in the system but doesn't directly weaken the statistical reduction if the system still works generally.
creator: Deepseek deepseek-v4-flash

prompt: A study followed 500 adults who consumed at least one cup of coffee daily for ten years. At the end of the study, those who drank coffee had a 20% lower incidence of heart disease compared to the general population. The researchers concluded that coffee consumption reduces the risk of heart disease. Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the researchers' conclusion?
choices: The coffee drinkers in the study were on average younger and more physically active than the general population.
The study controlled for factors such as age, gender, smoking, and exercise habits.
Many of the participants switched between regular and decaffeinated coffee during the study.
Other studies have found that coffee consumption is associated with higher cholesterol levels.
The participants self-reported their coffee intake, which may not be accurate.
exp: Controlling for confounding factors strengthens the conclusion that coffee itself is responsible for the lower heart disease risk. Choice A would weaken by suggesting the coffee drinkers were healthier to begin with. Choice D suggests a potential negative effect, which would weaken. Choice B is the correct strengthener.
creator: Deepseek deepseek-v4-flash

prompt: A company implemented a four-day workweek for its employees. After six months, employee satisfaction surveys improved by 30% and productivity remained the same. The CEO concluded that the four-day workweek improves employee well-being without harming productivity. The CEO's conclusion depends on assuming which of the following?
choices: The employees worked fewer total hours per week under the four-day schedule.
The improvement in satisfaction was not due to other changes the company made at the same time.
Productivity was measured using a method that accounts for the reduced hours.
Employees who were already satisfied with their jobs were more likely to support the change.
The four-day workweek will be permanent for all departments.
exp: The CEO attributes the satisfaction increase to the four-day workweek. If other changes occurred simultaneously, they could have caused the improvement, which would undermine the conclusion. The assumption is that there is no alternative cause. Choice C is about productivity measurement, but the conclusion covers both well-being and productivity; however, the assumption needed is about causation. Choice B is the necessary assumption.

prompt: Real estate agent: 'Houses in the Oakwood neighborhood have appreciated 50% in value over the past five years. Therefore, buying a house in Oakwood now is sure to be a profitable investment.' The reasoning is most vulnerable to the criticism that it
choices: assumes that past appreciation guarantees future appreciation.
fails to consider that other neighborhoods may have appreciated even more.
ignores the possibility that housing prices could decrease in the short term.
uses the word 'profitable' without defining it clearly.
does not account for the costs of maintaining a house.
exp: The agent assumes that past performance (50% appreciation) will continue in the future, which is a classic flaw: extrapolating from past trends. Choice A directly captures this. Choice C is also about future declines but is narrower; the main flaw is the assumption that the trend will continue. Choice A is the best description of the flawed reasoning.
creator: Deepseek deepseek-v4-flash

prompt: All of the company's regional managers have at least ten years of experience. Some regional managers are also vice presidents. No vice president has less than five years of experience. If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true?
choices: Some vice presidents have at least ten years of experience.
All regional managers who are vice presidents have at least ten years of experience.
People with less than five years of experience cannot be vice presidents.
Some employees with ten or more years of experience are not vice presidents.
All vice presidents are regional managers.
exp: From 'All regional managers have at least ten years' and 'Some regional managers are vice presidents', it follows that those regional managers who are vice presidents have at least ten years. Choice B is directly supported. Choice A could be true but not necessarily: there could be vice presidents who are not regional managers and have less than ten years (but at least five). Choice C is true (no VP <5 years), but it's not 'must be true' based on the given statements? Actually, 'No vice president has less than five years' implies that anyone with less than five years cannot be VP, so C is also true. However, the question asks 'must also be true' and both B and C are true. But note: The prompt expects one correct answer. Let's check: C is a direct restatement of 'No vice president has less than five years' so it's also true. But typically in inference questions, we look for what follows from combining statements. The combination of 'All regional managers have at least ten' and 'Some regional managers are VPs' gives B. C is just restating the third statement. Since the question says 'If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true?' C is already stated, so it's not 'also' true; it's given. The correct inference should be something not explicitly stated. Thus B is the correct answer. C is a trap because it seems true but it's already given.

prompt: A pharmaceutical company developed a new drug for lowering blood pressure. In a clinical trial, patients who took the drug for six months showed an average reduction in systolic blood pressure of 10 mmHg compared to a placebo group. The company's researchers concluded that the drug is effective at reducing blood pressure. Which of the following, if true, most weakens the researchers' conclusion?
choices: The placebo group showed an average reduction of 1 mmHg in systolic blood pressure.
The patients in the drug group also made significant lifestyle changes, such as diet and exercise, during the trial.
The drug group had a higher dropout rate than the placebo group due to side effects.
Blood pressure was measured only at the beginning and end of the trial, not at regular intervals.
The drug is known to interact with common heart medications, potentially causing dangerous side effects.
exp: The conclusion that the drug caused the blood pressure reduction is weakened if the drug group also made lifestyle changes known to lower blood pressure, because those changes could account for the difference. The placebo group's small reduction (choice A) actually strengthens the drug's effectiveness. Choice B is the correct weakener. Choice C introduces side effects but does not directly challenge causality. Choice D is a measurement issue but not as directly damning as an alternative cause. Choice E is about safety, not efficacy.

prompt: A recent study found that children who play a musical instrument for at least two years score higher on average in mathematics tests than children who do not play any instrument. The researchers concluded that learning to play a musical instrument improves mathematical ability. Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the researchers' conclusion?
choices: Children who play musical instruments tend to have parents who are more involved in their education.
The study controlled for factors such as socioeconomic status and overall academic performance.
Many children who play instruments also receive tutoring in mathematics.
The difference in math scores was small, though statistically significant.
The study was conducted over a single year, and the children's math scores were measured only once.
exp: Controlling for variables like socioeconomic status and overall academic performance rules out alternative explanations for the higher math scores, strengthening the causal link. Choice A and C provide alternative explanations, weakening the conclusion. Choice D does not strengthen the causal claim. Choice E is a weakness, not a strength.

prompt: A marketing firm advised a client to increase its social media advertising budget. The firm pointed out that last quarter, the client's competitors who increased their social media ad spending saw an average 20% increase in website traffic. The marketing firm concluded that increasing the client's social media ad spending would similarly boost website traffic. The conclusion depends on assuming which of the following?
choices: The client's website traffic is currently lower than that of its competitors.
The client's products are comparable to those of its competitors in terms of quality and price.
The competitors' increase in website traffic was caused by their increased social media ad spending.
The client's social media advertising strategy is similar to that of its competitors.
The competitors will not further increase their social media ad spending in the upcoming quarter.
exp: The conclusion that increasing ad spending will cause traffic increase relies on the assumption that the competitors' traffic increase was caused by their ad spending (and not by other factors). Without that causal link, the analogy is weak. Choice C correctly identifies this assumption. Choice D is tempting but less critical: even if strategies differ, the general principle might still hold. But the core assumption is that the observed correlation is causal.

prompt: All employees who have been with the company for more than five years are eligible for the management training program. Some employees in the marketing department are eligible for the management training program. No employee in the marketing department has been with the company for fewer than three years. If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true?
choices: Some employees in the marketing department have been with the company for more than five years.
All employees in the marketing department are eligible for the management training program.
Some employees who have been with the company for more than five years are not in the marketing department.
No employee who has been with the company for fewer than three years is in the marketing department.
All employees eligible for the management training program have been with the company for more than five years.
exp: The statements say no one in marketing has fewer than three years, so it must be true that no employee with fewer than three years is in marketing. Choice A is tempting but not necessarily true: the second statement says 'some' marketing employees are eligible, meaning at least one has >5 years, but it doesn't guarantee that any marketing employee has >5 years? Actually, eligibility requires >5 years, so 'some marketing employees are eligible' implies at least one marketing employee has >5 years. Wait, that would make A also must be true? Let's check carefully: 'All employees who have been with the company for more than five years are eligible' — so >5 years implies eligible. But the converse is not stated: eligible does not necessarily imply >5 years? Actually the first sentence: 'All employees who have been with the company for more than five years are eligible' means if >5 years then eligible. But it does not say that only those with >5 years are eligible. So it's possible that eligibility also extends to others? The statement is one-way. So 'Some marketing employees are eligible' does not necessarily mean they have >5 years; they could be eligible for some other reason. But wait, the statement says 'All employees who have been with the company for more than five years are eligible for the management training program.' It doesn't say that eligibility is exclusively for those with >5 years. So there could be other criteria. Therefore we cannot infer that some marketing employees have >5 years. So A is not necessarily true. Choice D is directly stated: 'No employee in the marketing department has been with the company for fewer than three years' means all marketing employees have at least 3 years, so any employee with <3 years cannot be in marketing. That is a logical contrapositive: if in marketing then not <3 years. So D must be true. Choice C is likely true but not necessarily: it's possible all >5-year employees are in marketing? If all >5-year employees are in marketing, then C would be false. But we don't know. So D is the only one guaranteed.

prompt: Manager: 'Our company recently implemented a new remote work policy. Since then, employee productivity has increased by 15% according to our metrics. Therefore, the remote work policy is the direct cause of the productivity increase.' The reasoning is most vulnerable to the criticism that it
choices: Assumes that a correlation between two events implies a causal relationship.
Relies on a sample that may not be representative of the entire company.
Fails to consider that productivity might have increased due to seasonal factors.
Ignores the possibility that employees may have been working longer hours at home.
Confuses the cause of an increase with its effect.
exp: The manager sees a correlation (remote work policy implemented and then productivity increased) and concludes causation, which is a classic flaw. Choice A correctly identifies this. Choice B is not supported; there's no mention of a sample. Choice C is a specific alternative explanation, but the flaw is the general logical leap from correlation to causation. Choice D is also an alternative explanation but less direct. Choice E is vague. A is the best description of the logical flaw.

prompt: A state government funded a program to provide free job training to unemployed workers. After the program's first year, the unemployment rate in the state fell by 2%. Government officials concluded that the job training program caused the decrease in unemployment. Which of the following, if true, most weakens the officials' conclusion?

A survey found that 80% of program participants felt the training was useful.
exp: The officials attribute the unemployment drop to the program. If unemployment also fell in states without the program, then that suggests a regional trend (e.g., economic recovery) caused the drop, not the program. This weakens the causal claim. Choice B might suggest the program targeted growing industries, which could actually strengthen the argument that the program helped, not weaken it. Others are irrelevant or supportive.

prompt: A recent study found that employees who sit for more than six hours per day have a 30% higher risk of developing heart disease than those who sit for less than three hours. The researchers concluded that prolonged sitting increases the risk of heart disease. Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the researchers' conclusion?
choices: The study controlled for other factors such as diet, exercise, and smoking.

Participants who reduced their sitting time during the study showed a corresponding decrease in heart disease risk markers.
Heart disease is one of the leading causes of death worldwide.
exp: Choice 3 provides direct evidence of a dose-response relationship: reducing sitting time lowers heart disease risk, which strongly supports the causal link. Choice A controls for confounders, which strengthens but is weaker than direct reversal evidence. Choice B introduces a potential confound (stress) that actually weakens. C and E are not directly strengthening the causal claim.

prompt: A company plans to reduce its carbon emissions by 50% over the next decade by replacing all of its gasoline-powered delivery trucks with electric vehicles. The company concludes that the switch will be financially beneficial. The company's conclusion depends on assuming which of the following?

The price of gasoline will not decrease significantly over the next decade.
The company's delivery routes will not change significantly over the next decade.
The electric vehicles will be able to carry the same load as the gasoline-powered trucks.
The company will be able to sell its gasoline-powered trucks for a reasonable price.
exp: The conclusion that savings ($12M) exceed cost ($10M) depends on fuel cost savings being realized. If gasoline prices drop, fuel savings might be less than expected, undermining the financial benefit. A is too strong; the argument assumes no unforeseen major costs, but minor maintenance is acceptable. C, D, and E are not necessary: route changes could affect fuel use but the savings estimate is based on current routes, and load capacity is not directly relevant to fuel savings.

prompt: A study of 1,000 adults found that those who drank red wine daily had a 15% lower risk of heart disease than those who never drank red wine. The lead researcher concluded that drinking red wine daily reduces the risk of heart disease. The reasoning is most vulnerable to the criticism that it
choices: assumes that correlation implies causation without considering other factors.
relies on a sample that is too small to draw a general conclusion.
overlooks the possibility that red wine drinkers may have healthier lifestyles in general.
fails to define what constitutes a 'daily' serving of red wine.
ignores the potential negative health effects of alcohol consumption.
exp: The study shows a correlation but does not account for confounding variables such as diet, exercise, or socioeconomic status. Red wine drinkers might engage in other healthy behaviors that actually reduce heart disease risk. This is a classic post hoc ergo propter hoc flaw. Choice A is a general statement that is true but less specific than C. The sample size (1000) is not too small. D and E are potential issues but not the primary flaw.

prompt: A city council is considering a ban on plastic shopping bags to reduce litter. Supporters of the ban point to a neighboring city that implemented a similar ban and saw a 40% reduction in plastic bag litter within one year. The answer to which of the following questions would be most useful in evaluating whether the ban is likely to have a similar effect in the council's city?
choices: Did the neighboring city also impose fees on paper bags?
What was the level of plastic bag litter in the council's city before the ban?
Did the neighboring city experience an increase in litter from other materials after the ban?
Are the demographics of the two cities similar?
How much plastic bag litter is generated by businesses versus households?
exp: To evaluate whether the ban will reduce overall litter, we need to know if the reduction in plastic bag litter was offset by increases in other types of litter (e.g., paper bags, reusable bags). If so, the net benefit might be smaller. Choice A is relevant but less direct because fees could affect behavior. B is about the baseline, but doesn't assess the ban's effectiveness. D and E are less critical than the substitution effect.
